topic: A neighbor wants to install a fence
raw_output:
[story 1]

The mail had arrived this morning, a thick stack of glossy flyers detailing the various fence materials available. Every picture showed a different style—rustic wood, sleek vinyl, chain link—and the sheer volume of choices made my chest tighten. I stared at the blueprints spread across the kitchen table, the lines blurring into an indecipherable mess. When Mr. Henderson knocked on my door, his smile was bright, the enthusiasm radiating off him like heat. "Just wanted to see if you were ready to get this project moving," he chirped. I managed a tight nod, my hands gripping the edge of the table until my knuckles were white. My throat felt suddenly dry, and I could only manage a weak, "Let me... think about it."

[story 2]

I kept pacing the length of the backyard, the gravel crunching under my worn sneakers with every step. The proposal from the neighbor was detailed, comprehensive, and frankly, exhausting to process. It was a cascade of specifications, measurements, and material costs that swam before my eyes. I kept rubbing my temples, a dull ache blooming behind my eyes. When he called again, his voice was upbeat, suggesting we could finalize the design by the end of the week. I stopped mid-stride, my shoulders slumping visibly. "I... I don't know where to start," I mumbled, the words catching in my throat. I looked down at my hands, suddenly feeling clumsy and incapable of making a single decision.

[story 3]

The sheer scale of the task seemed to press down on me, heavy and suffocating. I walked into the living room, intending to find a quiet corner, but the sheer number of options presented by the neighbor’s plans kept pulling my attention back to the paperwork. I picked up a pen, intending to sketch out a simple layout, but the lines looked chaotic, the space too vast. A wave of tension washed through me, making my jaw clench. When the neighbor appeared, gesturing toward the pile of documents, I felt a sudden need to retreat. "This is... a lot," I said, my voice coming out much quieter than I intended. I just stood there, unable to meet his gaze, feeling every minute detail of the impending construction settling squarely on my shoulders.
